ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 covers nacho food product as a category; no specific entry for a sharing-meal format with multiple toppings. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX covers corn-based snack foods; no specific code for nachos with toppings as a composite meal. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for nachos as a composite snack meal.

main_ingredients: cheddar cheese or processed cheese sauce
allergens: gluten (corn tortilla chips may contain wheat cross-contamination)

serving.natural_language_portion: A classic nachos sharing platter for two, typically comprising approximately 500 g total weight of tortilla chips layered with cheese sauce, salsa, sour cream, jalapeños, and optional toppings such as guacamole and black beans.

nutrient_source.title: Nachos, plain, tortilla chips with cheese — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry covers a full nachos sharing platter with all toppings. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for tortilla chips (NDB 19056), cheddar cheese (NDB 01009), sour cream (NDB 01056), salsa (NDB 06164), and jalapeño peppers (NDB 11979), weighted to a representative composite of approximately 45% tortilla chips, 25% cheese sauce, 15% sour cream, 10% salsa, and 5% jalapeños. This is a medium-confidence estimate. Per-100g values reflect the blended composite.

gi_source.title: Corn chips, plain, salted — Glycemic Index entry; tortilla/corn chip GI reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for a composite nachos sharing platter. GI of 63 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain corn/tortilla chips (GI approximately 63, medium category). The composite platter lowers effective GI slightly due to fat and protein from cheese and sour cream, but 63 is retained as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (63 × 175 g carbohydrate in 500 g serving) / 100 = 110.3. Carbohydrate per serving = 35.0 g per 100 g × 500 g / 100 = 175 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~2400 mg per 500 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid classic nachos platters due to very high sodium content from tortilla chips, processed cheese, and salsa, which can significantly exceed the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single shared serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load and medium glycemic index (GI 63) with very high glycemic load per serving (~110)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with classic nachos as the large serving size delivers a very high glycemic load (~110 GL units), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion control and pairing with high-fibre or protein-rich foods is strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~6.5 g per 100 g), high sodium, and trans fat presence (~0.2 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit classic nachos due to the combination of high saturated fat from cheese and sour cream, significant sodium from chips and salsa, and trace trans fats, all of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles and elevated cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Classic Nachos Meal for Two is a Western-origin sharing platter consisting of crispy tortilla chips (made from corn masa) layered generously with melted cheddar or processed cheese sauce, dollops of sour cream, chunky tomato salsa, sliced jalapeño peppers, and optional toppings such as guacamole and seasoned black beans. The dish is typically assembled by spreading chips on a large oven-safe tray, covering with cheese, and baking or grilling until the cheese melts, then finishing with cold toppings. The texture profile combines the crunch of the chips with the creamy richness of cheese and sour cream, the acidity of salsa, and the heat of jalapeños. Originating in northern Mexico and popularised across the United States, nachos are now a globally recognised snack and sharing meal format. In Singapore, nachos appear on menus at Western casual dining outlets and food courts, often priced as a meal deal for two. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (~320 kcal per 100 g), high in refined carbohydrates (35 g per 100 g), moderately high in fat (17 g per 100 g) with notable saturated fat from dairy, and high in sodium (~480 mg per 100 g). The glycemic index is medium (~63) but the glycemic load per full serving is very high (~110), making portion control critical for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is not suitable for vegan, halal-certified, or gluten-free diets without modification. It is a high-concern food for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, saturated fat, and carbohydrate density.
common_user_queries: How many calories are in nachos?
What is the GI of nachos?
How much sodium is in nachos?
Is nachos good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry covers a composite nachos sharing platter with all toppings. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual components (tortilla chips, cheddar cheese, sour cream, salsa, jalapeños) weighted to a representative composite. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain corn/tortilla chips. Confidence is medium because the composite estimation introduces uncertainty in exact macro and micronutrient values depending on restaurant-specific recipes and topping ratios.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for tortilla chips (NDB 19056), cheddar cheese (NDB 01009), sour cream (NDB 01056), salsa (NDB 06164), and jalapeño peppers (NDB 11979), weighted approximately 45:25:15:10:5. 2. The input record listed cuisine as 'Sandwich' and restaurants as Subway-Funan and Funan Mix & Match; however, the food name 'Classic Nachos Meal for 2' is clearly a Western tortilla-chip platter, not a sandwich. Cuisine has been corrected to 'Western' and region of origin set to 'United States'. 3. GI of 63 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain corn/tortilla chips; the composite platter may have a slightly lower effective GI due to fat and protein from dairy toppings. 4. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component USDA entries for this composite food. 5. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (63 × 175) / 100 = 110.3, where 175 g is the estimated carbohydrate content of a 500 g serving (35 g per 100 g × 5). 6. Halal status is listed as false because standard nachos include dairy from non-halal-certified sources and the restaurant context (Subway Singapore, Funan Mix & Match) does not guarantee halal certification for this specific item. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the USDA composite and carry medium confidence. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database entries used for this composite estimate.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected cuisine from 'Sandwich' (input error) to 'Western' based on food name analysis.
Added mandatory multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) appropriate for a Singapore-served Western dish.
Constructed composite nutrient estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries with documented weighting.
Set GI to 63 (medium) from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for corn/tortilla chips.
Calculated glycemic load per serving as 110.3 based on 500 g serving size.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 with explicit note that it is not reported in source database.
Ensured all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Set ontology match types to 'closest_parent' or 'none' only — no invented codes.
Added zero-macro justification rule check: no core macros are zero, rule not triggered.
Added sentinel value documentation for vitamin_d_mcg = -1 in notes field.
